prompt_template:
You are explaining science to a 7-year-old in the 3rd grade.

Rules:
- Every single sentence must be simple enough for an 8-year-old to read on their own. Use short sentences and common, everyday words.
- Avoid technical terms. If you must use one, explain it in plain words right away.
- Stay scientifically accurate. Explain HOW or WHY it really works — the actual reason — not just what it is.
- Do NOT oversimplify so much that it becomes wrong.
- Write 4 to 8 sentences.

Explain: {CONCEPT}

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: Why is the sky blue?
records.text:
Sunlight looks white, but it holds all the colors of a rainbow.  
The air is made of tiny bits called molecules.  
When sunlight reaches these bits, they send the light in many directions.  
Blue light has shorter waves, so it gets sent around more than red light.  
That blue light reaches our eyes from all across the sky.  
Some purple light spreads too, but our eyes see blue more easily.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: How do plants make their own food?
records.text:
Plants make food in their leaves by a process called photosynthesis.  
Their roots take in water from the soil.  
Their leaves take in a gas called carbon dioxide from the air.  
A green material in the leaves catches energy from sunlight.  
The plant uses this energy to turn the water and gas into sugar.  
The sugar helps the plant grow, and oxygen goes back into the air.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: Why do we have day and night?
records.text:
Earth is shaped like a ball and spins around.  
The Sun lights up only the side of Earth that faces it.  
That bright side has day.  
The side facing away from the Sun is dark, so it has night.  
Earth takes about 24 hours to spin around one time.  
As Earth spins, your home moves into sunlight and then into darkness.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: What makes ice melt?
records.text:
Ice melts when it takes in heat from things around it.  
The heat makes the tiny water pieces in the ice move faster.  
In solid ice, these pieces stay in a fixed pattern.  
As they gain heat, they shake more and break out of that pattern.  
Then the solid ice becomes liquid water.  
Ice usually melts when it warms to about 32°F, or 0°C.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: How do magnets work?
records.text:
Everything is made of tiny pieces called atoms.  
Atoms have even smaller parts called electrons.  
Each electron makes a tiny magnetic force around itself.  
In most things, these forces point in many ways and cancel out.  
In a magnet, many of the forces line up and work together.  
This makes an unseen magnetic field that can push or pull.  
Different ends pull together, but matching ends push apart.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: Why do things fall to the ground?
records.text:
Things fall because Earth pulls them toward its center.  
This pull is called gravity.  
All things pull on each other, but Earth is very big.  
So its pull makes things move down toward the ground.  
The falling thing also pulls on Earth, but Earth is too big to move much.  
The ground stops the thing when they push against each other.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: Where does a puddle go when it dries up?
records.text:
Some water may soak into the ground.  
The rest goes up into the air.  
Heat from the sun makes the water move faster.  
Then it can leave the puddle as an invisible gas.  
This gas is called water vapor.  
Moving air carries the water vapor away.  
The water can later form clouds and fall as rain.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: Why do we have seasons?
records.text:
We have seasons because Earth is tilted as it moves around the Sun.  
For part of the year, our half of Earth tilts toward the Sun.  
Sunlight hits us more straight on, and the days are longer.  
This gives us more heat, so we have summer.  
When our half tilts away, sunlight is weaker and the days are shorter, so we have winter.  
Spring and fall happen as the tilt changes from one to the other.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: How do our lungs help us breathe?
records.text:
Your lungs are two soft, stretchy organs inside your chest.  
When you breathe in, a strong muscle under your lungs pulls down.  
This makes more room in your chest, so air flows into your lungs.  
Tiny air sacs in your lungs move oxygen from the air into your blood.  
Your blood carries this oxygen to every part of your body.  
When you breathe out, your lungs push out carbon dioxide, a waste gas made by your body.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: What makes a rainbow?
records.text:
A rainbow forms when sunlight shines through many drops of water.  
Sunlight looks white, but it holds many colors.  
When the light enters a drop, it bends because water slows it down.  
Some light bounces off the back of the drop.  
The light bends again as it leaves the drop.  
Each color bends by a different amount, so the colors spread apart.  
We see an arc because only certain drops send each color into our eyes.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: Why does the moon look like it changes shape?
records.text:
The Moon does not really change shape.  
It is always a round ball.  
The Sun lights up half of the Moon.  
As the Moon moves around Earth, we see different parts of its bright half.  
Sometimes we see a thin bright slice, and sometimes we see a full bright circle.  
Earth’s shadow does not cause these changes.

records.label: GPT-5.6 SOL
records.concept: How do fish breathe underwater?
records.text:
Fish breathe with body parts called gills.  
A fish takes in water through its mouth.  
The water has tiny bits of oxygen gas mixed in it.  
The fish moves the water over its thin gills.  
Oxygen moves through the gills and into the fish’s blood.  
A waste gas called carbon dioxide moves from the blood into the water.  
Then the water flows out through openings near the gills.

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: Why is the sky blue?
records.text: Sunlight looks white, but it is really made of many colors mixed together. When sunlight hits the air, it bumps into tiny bits of gas floating all around us. Blue light bounces off these tiny bits much more than the other colors do. This bouncing is called scattering, and it spreads the blue light all across the sky. So when you look up, you see blue light coming at you from every direction! At sunset, the sun is low, so its light travels through more air to reach you. By then, most of the blue has bounced away, and you see the reds and oranges that are left.

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: How do plants make their own food?
records.text:
Plants make their food using sunlight, water, and air. Their roots drink up water from the dirt. Their leaves take in a gas from the air called carbon dioxide, which is the same gas we breathe out.

Inside the leaves are tiny green bits that catch sunlight, kind of like tiny solar panels. The sunlight gives the plant energy to mix the water and gas together. This mixing makes a sugar, and that sugar is the plant's food!

The plant also makes oxygen while it works, and it puffs that out into the air for us to breathe. Pretty cool, right?

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: Why do we have day and night?
records.text: We have day and night because the Earth spins like a top. The Earth is a big ball, and it turns all the way around one time each day. The Sun stays in one spot and shines on the Earth. When your side of the Earth is facing the Sun, it is day where you are. When your side turns away from the Sun, it is night. It takes 24 hours for the Earth to spin all the way around, which is why one day and one night together last that long.

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: What makes ice melt?
records.text: Ice melts when it gets warm. Ice is water that is frozen hard and stiff. The tiny bits inside ice, called molecules, are locked together and can barely move. When heat touches the ice, it gives those tiny bits more energy. That energy makes them wiggle and shake faster. Soon they wiggle so much that they break free and slide around. When that happens, the hard ice turns into runny water!

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: How do magnets work?
records.text:
Magnets work because of tiny bits inside them called electrons. Electrons spin, and when they spin, they make a small pull called a magnetic force. In most things, the electrons spin in all different ways, so their pulls cancel out. But in a magnet, lots of electrons spin the same way, so their pulls add up into one strong force.

This force can pull on iron and a few other metals. Every magnet has two ends, called the north and south poles. Opposite poles pull each other close, but two of the same poles push each other away!

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: Why do things fall to the ground?
records.text: Things fall because Earth pulls on them. This pull is called gravity. Everything with stuff in it pulls on other things, but big things pull much harder. Earth is huge, so it pulls everything toward its center. That is why a ball drops down when you let go. You are being pulled too, which is why your feet stay on the ground. Even you pull on the Earth a tiny bit, but you are way too small to move it!

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: Where does a puddle go when it dries up?
records.text: When a puddle dries up, the water does not really disappear. It turns into tiny bits so small you can't see them, and those bits float up into the air. This is called evaporation. The sun warms the water and gives it energy. That energy makes the water bits move faster and faster until they zoom right out of the puddle and into the sky. Later, way up high, these bits can join together to make clouds. So the puddle is still around — it just went on a trip into the air!

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: Why do we have seasons?
records.text:
Earth is tilted a little bit, like a spinning top that leans to one side. As Earth moves around the Sun each year, this tilt stays pointing the same way. So sometimes your part of Earth leans toward the Sun, and sometimes it leans away.

When your part leans toward the Sun, the Sun's light hits you more straight on. That strong, direct light heats the ground more, and the days are longer too. That's summer! When your part leans away, the light hits at a slant and spreads out, so it feels cooler and days are shorter. That's winter.

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: How do our lungs help us breathe?
records.text: Your lungs are like two soft, squishy bags inside your chest. When you breathe in, a strong muscle under your lungs, called the diaphragm, pulls down. This makes more room in your chest, so air rushes in through your nose or mouth to fill the space. Inside your lungs are tiny air pockets, kind of like little balloons. Around these pockets are tiny tubes of blood. The air pockets pass oxygen (the part of air your body needs) into your blood, and take out a waste gas called carbon dioxide. When you breathe out, the muscle pushes back up and squeezes that waste gas out of your body!

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: What makes a rainbow?
records.text: A rainbow happens when sunlight shines through tiny raindrops in the sky. Sunlight looks white, but it is really made of many colors mixed together. When light goes into a raindrop, it slows down and bends a little. Each color bends by a different amount, so they spread out and split apart. The light then bounces off the back of the raindrop and comes back out. That is why you see a curve of colors, with red on top and purple on the bottom. You need the sun behind you and rain in front of you to spot one!

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: Why does the moon look like it changes shape?
records.text: The moon does not really change shape. It only looks different because of sunlight. The sun always shines on one side of the moon, just like it shines on Earth. As the moon moves around Earth, we see different parts of its sunny side. Sometimes we see all of the sunny side, and the moon looks like a big round circle. Other times we only see a little sliver of the sunny side, and the rest is dark. The dark part is still there, but we can't see it because no sunlight is hitting it.

records.label: Claude Opus 4.7
records.concept: How do fish breathe underwater?
records.text: Fish need oxygen to live, just like you do. But instead of getting it from the air, they pull it out of the water. Fish have special body parts called gills on the sides of their heads. When a fish takes a gulp of water through its mouth, the water flows over the gills. The gills are full of tiny blood vessels, which are little tubes that carry blood. Oxygen slips out of the water and into the blood through the thin walls of these tubes. Then the water flows out through slits behind the gills, and the blood carries the oxygen all around the fish's body.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: Why is the sky blue?
records.text: Light from the sun looks white, but it is really made of all the colors of the rainbow. This light travels to Earth in tiny waves. Some colors travel in long waves, but blue light travels in short, choppy waves. High above us, the air is full of tiny, invisible bits of gas. When sunlight hits these bits of gas, the short blue waves bounce off them. The blue light scatters, which means it flies out in every direction across the sky. When you look up, your eyes catch all that bouncing blue light!

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: How do plants make their own food?
records.text: Plants use their green leaves like tiny kitchens to make their own food. They need sunlight, water, and a type of air called carbon dioxide. The plant pulls water up from the dirt and catches sunlight with its leaves. It also breathes in the carbon dioxide through tiny hidden holes. The plant uses the sun's energy to mix the water and carbon dioxide together. This mixing changes them into a sweet sugar that the plant uses to live and grow. When the plant is done making sugar, it lets out fresh air called oxygen for us to breathe.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: Why do we have day and night?
records.text: We live on a giant ball called Earth. The sun shines bright light onto our planet. However, the sun can only light up one half of the Earth at a time. Earth is always spinning around like a very slow top. When the side we live on turns toward the sun, we get daytime. As Earth keeps spinning, our side turns away from the sun. Then we are in the dark, which is what we call night.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: What makes ice melt?
records.text: Ice is made of tiny pieces of water that are locked tightly together. These tiny pieces are always wiggling just a little bit. When the air around the ice is warm, it gives heat to the ice. Heat is a kind of energy that makes the tiny pieces wiggle much faster. Soon, they are shaking so hard that they break out of their locked spots. Once they break free, the pieces start sliding all around each other. When the pieces can flow and slide, the hard ice turns into liquid water.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: How do magnets work?
records.text: Everything in the world is made of super tiny pieces called atoms. Inside these atoms are even tinier pieces called electrons that are always spinning. When an electron spins, it creates a very small invisible force. In most things, these tiny pieces spin in all different directions and cancel each other out. But inside a magnet, all the spinning pieces line up to face the exact same way. Because they all work together, their tiny forces add up into one big force. This big invisible force is what makes a magnet stick to metal!

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: Why do things fall to the ground?
records.text: Things fall to the ground because of an invisible pull called gravity. Everything in the universe pulls on everything else. Big things have a much stronger pull than small things. The Earth is a giant ball of rock. Because the Earth is so huge, its pull is very strong. It pulls you and your toys straight toward its center. When you let go of a ball, the giant Earth pulls it down to the ground.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: Where does a puddle go when it dries up?
records.text: When a puddle dries up, the water does not just disappear. Water is made of tiny pieces that are always wiggling around. When the sun warms the puddle, these tiny pieces get lots of energy. They start to wiggle and bounce much faster. Soon, they bounce so fast that they pop right out of the puddle. The water turns into an invisible gas called water vapor. This invisible gas floats up and mixes into the air around us.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: Why do we have seasons?
records.text: The Earth is like a spinning ball that leans to one side. As our planet travels around the sun, it always stays leaning over. Because of this lean, your half of the Earth points toward the sun for part of the year. When your half points toward the sun, the light hits it straight on and makes it hot for summer. Later in the year, your half points away from the sun. Then the sunlight hits the ground on a slant. This slanted light spreads the heat out, and that makes it cold for winter.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: How do our lungs help us breathe?
records.text: Your lungs are like two stretchy balloons inside your chest. A big muscle under your lungs pulls down to suck air into your body. Inside your lungs, the air goes into millions of tiny air bubbles. These bubbles take a helpful gas called oxygen from the air and put it into your blood. Your blood carries this oxygen everywhere to give your body energy. Next, your blood drops off a waste gas that your body does not need. Finally, your big muscle pushes up to squeeze that waste gas out of your lungs.

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: What makes a rainbow?
records.text: Sunlight looks white, but it is actually made of all the colors mixed together. When it rains, tiny drops of water float in the air. Sunlight shines into these water drops and bends. Bending the light makes the hidden colors pull apart. The colors bounce off the back of the water drop and come back out. Each color bends a little differently, so they line up in a perfect pattern. This beautiful pattern of light is what we call a rainbow!

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: Why does the moon look like it changes shape?
records.text: The moon does not make its own light. It shines because light from the sun bounces off it. The sun always lights up exactly half of the moon. The moon is also moving in a big circle around our Earth. As the moon moves, we see different parts of its bright side. Sometimes we see the whole bright half, which looks like a big round circle. Other times we only see a tiny slice of the bright part. The moon never really changes shape, we just see different amounts of its sunny side!

records.label: Gemini 3.1 Pro
records.concept: How do fish breathe underwater?
records.text: Fish need an invisible gas called oxygen to live, just like you do. Instead of breathing air, fish get their oxygen from the water. First, a fish opens its mouth to gulp water inside. The water flows over special body parts called gills, which look like tiny red feathers. These gills are full of blood. As water washes over the gills, the blood pulls the oxygen right out of the water. Finally, the leftover water flows back out through openings on the fish's head.
